1: The superscription reads שִׁיר חֲנֻכַּת הַבַּיִת לְדָוִד (shîr ḥanukkāt habbayit lĕdāwid, "A Song for the Dedication of the Temple, of David") — ḥanukkāh: dedication (from ḥānak: to initiate, to dedicate). The same word as the Feast of Dedication (Hanukkah) in John 10:22. The verb אֲרוֹמִמְךָ (ʾărômimkā, "I exalt you") — rûm (hiphil: to lift up, to exalt): the Psalmist "lifts up" God in response to God having "lifted him up" — דִּלִּיתָנִי (dillîtānî, "you have lifted me up") from dālāh: to draw up (as drawing water from a well). The rescue is like drawing someone up from a cistern.
2: אֱלֹהַי שִׁוַּעְתִּי אֵלֶיךָ וַתִּרְפָּאֵנִי (ʾĕlōhay shiwwaʿtî ʾêlêkā wattirpāʾênî, "I cried to you for help, and you healed me") — shāwaʿ: to cry for help (same root as the prayer-cry in Ps 28:2); rāpāʾ: to heal. The healing may be literal recovery from illness or a metaphor for deliverance from life-threatening danger (v3 suggests near-death). The divine name ʾĕlōhay (my God) — the first-person possessive relationship as the ground for the cry.
3: יְהוָה הֶעֱלִיתָ מִן-שְׁאוֹל נַפְשִׁי (YHWH heʿĕlîtā min-shĕʾôl napshî, "LORD, you brought my soul up from the realm of the dead") — shĕʾôl: the underworld, the comprehensive OT term for the abode of the departed. חִיִּיתַנִי מִיּוֹרְדֵי-בוֹר (ḥiyyîtanî miyôrĕdê-bôr, "you restored my life from among those who descend to the pit") — bôr: cistern/pit (as in Ps 28:1) — a second term for the underworld focused on its depth and darkness. Ḥāyāh (piel: to keep alive, to give life): God preserving the Psalmist from joining the descent.
4: זַמְּרוּ לַיהוָה חֲסִידָיו (zammĕrû laYHWH ḥăsîdāyw, "Sing praises to the LORD, O you his faithful ones") — zāmar: to make music with instruments — the word behind mizmôr (psalm). Ḥăsîdîm: the faithful/loyal ones — the recipients of God's ḥesed (covenant love) who in turn are loyal to him. לְזֵכֶר קָדְשׁוֹ (lĕzêker qodshô, "and give thanks to his holy name") — zêker: memory, memorial — the "memory of his holiness" as what is acknowledged in thanksgiving.
5: כִּי רֶגַע בְּאַפּוֹ חַיִּים בִּרְצוֹנוֹ (kî regaʿ bĕʾappô ḥayyîm birṣônô, "For his anger lasts only a moment, but his favor lasts a lifetime") — regaʿ: a moment, an instant — the briefest possible time unit. ʾAp (nostril/anger) contrasted with rāṣôn (favor, pleasure, delight). לָעֶרֶב יָלִין בֶּכִי וְלַבֹּקֶר רִנָּה (lāʿereb yālîn bekî wĕlabbōqer rinnāh, "weeping may remain through the night, but joy arrives with the morning") — yālîn: to lodge, to spend the night as a temporary guest. Weeping spends the night but is gone by morning; rinnāh (the ringing shout of joy) comes with dawn.
6: וַאֲנִי אָמַרְתִּי בְשַׁלְוִי (waʾănî ʾāmartî bĕshalwî, "When I was prosperous, I said") — shalwāh: ease, tranquility, prosperity. The Psalmist's confession: prosperity breeds presumption. בַּל-אֶמּוֹט לְעוֹלָם (bal-ʾemmôṭ lĕʿôlām, "I will never be shaken") — māṭ: to totter, to be shaken, to slip. The confident claim of permanent stability — precisely the kind of claim that invites divine testing (vv7b-10). The false security of prosperity not grounded in divine favor.
7: יְהוָה בִּרְצוֹנְךָ הֶעֱמַדְתָּה לְהַרְרִי-עֹז (YHWH birṣônĕkā heʿĕmadtāh lĕharrî-ʿōz, "By your favor, LORD, you had made my mountain stand firm") — the Psalmist now acknowledges what he failed to see in v6: the stability was not inherent but the product of divine rāṣôn (favor). הִסְתַּרְתָּ פָנֶיךָ הָיִיתִי נִבְהָל (histartā pānêkā hāyîtî nibhāl, "when you hid your face, I was terrified") — the theology of the hidden God: hester pānim (hiding of the face) = YHWH withdrawing active presence. Bāhal (niphal: to be terrified): the consequence of divine withdrawal is existential dismay.
8: אֵלֶיךָ יְהוָה אֶקְרָא (ʾêlêkā YHWH ʾeqrāʾ, "To you, LORD, I called out") — the natural response to the divine face-hiding is not despair but prayer. Qārāʾ (to call, to cry out): the same call of vv2-3 that was heard and healed. The Psalmist did not turn to other sources of help when the divine face was hidden; he called more urgently to the one who hid it.
9: מַה-בֶּצַע בְּדָמִי (māh-beṣaʿ bĕdāmî, "What is gained if I die?") — beṣaʿ: gain, profit (often of commercial gain). The argument from divine interest: what does God gain from the Psalmist's death? הֲיוֹדֶה עָפָר אֱמִתֶּךָ (hăyôdeh ʿāpār ʾĕmittekā, "Can the dust praise you? Can it proclaim your faithfulness?") — ʿāpār (dust): what the dead return to (Gen 3:19). The implicit argument: the living praise; the dead are silent. God's interest in his own glory is best served by keeping the Psalmist alive — not manipulation but the logic of covenant.
10: שְׁמַע-יְהוָה וְחָנֵּנִי (shĕmaʿ-YHWH wĕḥonnênî, "Hear me, LORD, and have mercy on me!") — ḥānan: to be gracious, to show mercy/favor (same root as taḥanûn in Ps 28:2). The plea for ḥēn (grace): unearned divine favor. יְהוָה הֱיֵה-עֹזֵר לִי (YHWH hĕyêh-ʿōzêr lî, "LORD, be my helper!") — the simple, direct appeal in the moment of need.
11: הָפַכְתָּ מִסְפְּדִי לְמָחוֹל לִי (hāpaktā mispĕdî lĕmāḥôl lî, "You have turned my mourning into dancing") — hāpak: to turn, to overturn, to reverse — the same reversal-word used for the overturning of Sodom (Gen 19:25). Mispēd: mourning, formal lamentation. Māḥôl: circle-dancing (from ḥûl: to whirl in circles) — exuberant communal celebration. פִּתַּחְתָּ שַׂקִּי וַתְּאַזְּרֵנִי שִׂמְחָה (pittaḥtā śaqqî wattĕʾazzĕrênî śimḥāh, "you have removed my sackcloth and clothed me with joy") — śaq: sackcloth — the rough mourning-garment; śimḥāh (joy) as the new garment. Joy as something worn, not merely felt.
12: לְמַעַן יְזַמֶּרְךָ כָבוֹד וְלֹא יִדֹּם (lĕmaʿan yĕzammĕrĕkā kābôd wĕlōʾ yiddōm, "so that my spirit may sing your praise and not be silent") — kābôd here may mean "my honor/my soul" (the internal, honored self) rather than "glory." Yiddōm (to be silent, to be still): the counterpart to the silence of death (v9). The rescued soul cannot be silent — silence is for the dead. יְהוָה אֱלֹהַי לְעוֹלָם אוֹדֶךָּ (YHWH ʾĕlōhay lĕʿôlām ʾôdekā, "O LORD my God, I will give you thanks forever") — the closing vow: forever-praise as the response to the forever-favor of v5.
